content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-7-max

## Summary

Reviewed the bead-materialization feature (materialize_beads in service.rs plus the milestone export-beads CLI). The ADD-only direction is respected, but the idempotency and scoping contracts are incomplete. Most critically, comments are re-posted on every rerun (violating the explicit "running twice doesn't create duplicates" acceptance criterion), and title-only matching against br list-all silently reuses same-titled beads from other milestones. Two smaller correctness issues: implicit-id proposals break depends_on resolution, and export-beads emits no journal event for traceability. Findings are scoped to the new code in service.rs and cli/milestone.rs; orchestration-state files under .ralph-burning/ were intentionally skipped.

## Amendments

1. Comment writes in `materialize_beads` are not idempotent. In `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1552-1563`, the workstream description is unconditionally posted via `comment_bead(&workstream_epic.id, description)` on every call — even when the workstream epic was just matched (`created = false`). Likewise the per-bead planning rationale at lines 1591-1604 is posted every time `render_bead_planning_comment(...)` returns `Some`, with no guard against the bead being reused. The `materialize_beads_is_idempotent_for_existing_titles` test at lines 15156-15245 actually *codifies* this behavior: on a full rerun it accepts 8 fresh "comment added" mutations even though `created_beads == 0`. This directly violates the acceptance criterion "Idempotent: running twice doesn't create duplicates" and will accumulate duplicate rationale comments on every subsequent export (e.g. when one new bead is added to the plan and the user reruns export-beads, every previously-exported bead gets a second copy of its rationale). Gate the two `comment_bead` calls on `created == true` (or diff against the existing comment set via `br comments list --json`) so reused beads skip comment reposting. Update the idempotent test to assert zero comment mutations on rerun.
   Rationale: The task's explicit acceptance criterion requires idempotent reruns. The current code and its test both accept duplicate comments, so every rerun pollutes the tracker with duplicate rationale/description comments.
2. Cross-milestone title collisions silently reuse unrelated beads. `materialize_beads` builds `existing_by_title` from `BrCommand::list_all()` (`src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1491-1504`), which returns every bead in the repo. `materialize_bead_entry` (lines 1659-1694) then pops a match by normalized title alone — no filter by milestone, label, or type. Consequences: (1) the root epic is materialized with `bundle.identity.name` (e.g. "Alpha") as its title and no scoping, so a pre-existing bead titled "Alpha" from an unrelated milestone is reused as this milestone's root; (2) workstream epics use generic names like "Core" or "Validation" that are highly likely to collide across milestones — the exporter will link this milestone's new tasks under another milestone's "Core" epic; (3) the created root is labeled `milestone-root` but the match ignores labels, so the label does not protect against misreuse. Recommended: label every materialized bead with a milestone-scoped label (e.g. `milestone:<id>`) and filter `existing_by_title` to entries carrying that label before matching. Add a test where two milestones share a workstream name and the second export does not rebind to the first's epic.
   Rationale: The idempotency guard is the only correctness backstop for repeated exports, but its scope is wrong — it can bind a milestone's hierarchy to beads owned by another milestone, silently corrupting the tracker. This is exactly the "duplicates" failure mode the acceptance criterion tries to prevent, just in the opposite direction.
3. Implicit bead IDs in `depends_on` cause export to fail. In `src/contexts/milestone_record/service.rs:1606-1608`, `proposal_id_map` is populated only when `proposal.bead_id.is_some()`. The final dependency loop at lines 1617-1644 looks up `proposal_id_map.get(&dependency)` and errors with "bead depends on unknown proposal id" on miss. However, `MilestoneBundle::validate` in Compatibility mode (`src/contexts/milestone_record/bundle.rs:91-410`) accepts proposals without an explicit `bead_id` — they get an implicit identity like `<milestone>.bead-N` and may be referenced from other proposals' `depends_on` using that implicit form. Running `materialize_beads` on such a valid bundle will fail mid-way (after creating root + workstream epics + some tasks) with no sync flush. Fix: when building `proposal_id_map`, also register the implicit canonical bead id (same form used by `backfill_legacy_explicit_bead_flags` / `normalize_bead_reference`), and have the dependency loop normalize each `depends_on` reference through the same function before lookup. Add a test bundle that uses implicit IDs in `depends_on`.
   Rationale: `load_plan_bundle` runs compatibility-mode validation, so any plan that predates or omits explicit bead_ids is a valid input to export-beads. The exporter will fail on the final dep loop and leave a partially-created, unflushed hierarchy behind.
4. `handle_export_beads` records nothing in the milestone journal. `src/cli/milestone.rs:360-380` creates beads, comments, and dependency edges against `br`, then sets the active milestone and prints a summary — but it never appends a `MilestoneJournalEvent` recording that an export happened, what root/workstream/task IDs were produced, or which bundle hash was exported. Other mutating milestone commands (plan/run/next) keep a journal trail. Without an event here, forensics after a failed or partial export (e.g., to reconcile which IDs `br` owns for this milestone) has to reverse-engineer state from the `br` tracker alone. Consider appending a `BeadsExported` (or similar) journal event with the bundle hash and the `BeadMaterializationReport` details before returning.
   Rationale: The existing project convention is to journal milestone-level mutations. Skipping it here leaves the milestone record desynced from the bead graph it produced — which is especially painful because the export can partially succeed (some beads created, then failure leaves the rest).
